html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一车 - Hexagram '車' (chariot) in the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), associated with unusual or strange phenomena.
  • 南淇之奥 - Poem 'Nan Qi' in the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), described as playful or humorous.
  • 固服缝掖者 - Refers to scholars who wear the traditional robes of Confucian scholars.
  • 肆笔之余 - Writing in their spare time.
  • 及怪及戏 - Including both strange and playful elements.
  • 无侵于儒 - Not infringing on the seriousness of Confucianism.
  • 无若诗书之味大羹 - The taste of poetry and books is like a rich broth.
  • 史为折俎 - History is like a dissected animal.
  • 子为酰醢 - The *Zi* (various philosophical texts) are like vinegar and soy sauce.
  • 炙羞鳖 - Metaphor comparing the enjoyment of different types of writing to eating roasted birds and turtles.
  • 岂容下箸乎 - How could one not enjoy them?
  • 固役而不耻者 - Those who work diligently without shame.
  • 抑志怪小说之书也 - Books that record strange and novel stories.
  • 成式学落词曼 - The author's writing style is somewhat loose and flowing.
  • 未尝覃思 - He has not deeply pondered.
  • 无崔真龙之叹 - He does not have the lament of Cui Zhenlong.
  • 有孔璋画虎之讥 - He is criticized for painting tigers like Kong Zhang.
  • 饱食之暇 - In the spare time after eating.
  • 偶录记忆 - Occasionally recording memories.
  • 号《酉阳杂俎》 - Naming it 'Yuyang Zazhu'.
  • 凡三十篇，为二十卷 - It consists of thirty chapters in twenty volumes.
  • 不以此间录味也 - Not to record the taste of the time.
  • 高祖少神勇 - Emperor Gaozu of Tang, known for his bravery.
  • 尝以十二人破草贼号无端儿数万 - Once defeated tens of thousands of bandits with just twelve men.
  • 又龙门战，尽一房箭，中八十人 - Another battle at Longmen, using all the arrows in one quiver, hitting eighty people.
  • 太宗虬须，尝戏张弓挂矢 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, with a curly beard, once played with a bow and arrow.
  • 好用四羽大，长常箭一肤，射洞门阖 - Liked using large arrows with four feathers, the arrow was as long as a skin, shooting through the door and hitting the latch.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《易》 - The *Yijing* (Book of Changes), one of the Five Classics.
  • 段成式 - A Tang dynasty writer and scholar, known for his works on strange and novel stories.
  • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Tang, the founder of the Tang dynasty.
  • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, a famous emperor known for his military prowess and administrative skills.
  • 崔真龙 - A Tang dynasty poet known for his lamentations.
  • 孔璋 - Kong Zhang, a historical figure known for his painting skills.

Proper Nouns

  • 《易》 - The *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
  • 段成式 - A Tang dynasty writer and scholar.
  • 高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
  • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
  • 崔真龙 - A Tang dynasty poet.
  • 孔璋 - Kong Zhang, a historical figure.
  • 《酉阳杂俎》 - The title of the book being discussed.
  • 龙门 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in the Tang dynasty.
  • 无端儿 - A term for bandits or rebels.
  • 四羽大 - A type of arrow with four feathers.
  • 肤 - A unit of length, possibly referring to the length of an arrow.
  • 洞门阖 - A door and its latch.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 上嘗觀漁於西宮 - The Emperor once observed fishing at the Western Palace; '嘗' indicates a past event, and '觀漁' can imply a symbolic or ritualistic act.
  • 見魚躍焉 - He saw the fish leap; '焉' indicates location or a completed action in the context of the observation.
  • 此當乳也 - This is because they are about to give birth; '當乳' is a technical term referring to the act of giving birth, specifically in the context of fish.
  • 中網而止 - The net was cast and the fishing stopped; '中網' means 'the net was cast,' and '而止' indicates the cessation of the action.
  • 骨利干國獻馬百疋 - The country of Bolqan presented a hundred horses; '獻' means 'to present' or 'to offer,' and '百疋' is a specific quantity, 'a hundred horses,' where '疋' is the unit for horses.
  • 十疋尤駿 - Ten of them were especially swift; '尤駿' means 'especially swift' or 'particularly fast.'
  • 上為制名決波輸者 - The Emperor named them 'Juebo Shu'; '制名' means 'to name' or 'to give a name,' and '決波輸者' is the name given to the horse, possibly referring to its agility or speed.
  • 近後足有距 - Near the hind legs, they have spurs; '距' refers to spurs, which are bony projections on the legs of certain animals, often used for defense or mating.
  • 走歷門三限不躓 - They can run through three thresholds without stumbling; '走' means 'to run,' '歷' means 'to pass through,' '門三限' refers to three thresholds or gates, and '不躓' means 'without stumbling.'
  • 上尤惜之 - The Emperor especially cherished them; '尤惜' means 'especially cherished' or 'treasured greatly.'
  • 隋內庫有交臂玉猿 - The Sui dynasty's treasury had a jade monkey with interlocking arms; '交臂' means 'interlocking arms,' and '玉猿' refers to a jade statue of a monkey.
  • 二臂相貫如連環 - Its two arms were joined like a linked ring; '相貫' means 'joined together,' and '連環' means 'linked ring.'
  • 將表其轡 - He intended to attach it to the reins; '表' means 'to attach' or 'to adorn,' and '轡' refers to the reins of a horse.
  • 上後嘗騎與侍臣遊 - The Emperor later rode with his attendants for a tour; '後' means 'later,' and '遊' means 'to tour' or 'to travel.'
  • 惡其飾 - He disliked its ornamentation; '惡' means 'to dislike,' and '飾' refers to the ornamentation or decoration.
  • 以鞭擊碎之 - He struck it with a whip and broke it; '擊碎' means 'to strike and break.'
  • 則天初誕之夕 - On the night of Wu Zetian's birth; '初誕' means 'birth,' and '夕' refers to the evening or night.
  • 雌雉皆ず - All the hens crouched; 'ず' is a verb meaning 'to crouch' or 'to squat,' often used to describe birds.
  • 右手中指有黑毫 - There was a black hair on her right middle finger; '黑毫' refers to a black hair, often considered a sign of good fortune or special destiny.
  • 左旋如黑子 - It twisted to the left like a black mole; '左旋' means 'to twist to the left,' and '黑子' refers to a mole or dark spot on the skin.
  • 引之尺余 - It could be pulled out over a foot; '引之' means 'to pull it,' and '尺余' means 'over a foot' or 'more than a foot.'
  • 高宗初扶床 - When Gaozong was just learning to walk; '初扶床' means 'just learning to walk' or 'just starting to support himself on a bed.'
  • 將戲弄筆 - He was playing with a pen; '戲弄' means 'to play with' or 'to fiddle with.'
  • 左右試置紙於前 - The attendants placed paper in front of him; '試置' means 'to place' or 'to put.'
  • 乃亂畫滿紙 - He scribbled all over the paper; '亂畫' means 'to scribble' or 'to draw randomly.'
  • 太宗遽令焚之 - Emperor Taizong immediately ordered it to be burned; '遽令' means 'immediately ordered,' and '焚之' means 'to burn it.'
  • 不許傳外 - He did not allow it to be spread outside; '不許' means 'not allowed,' and '傳外' means 'to spread outside.'

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 中宗景龙中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang during the Jinglong era.
  • 作吐陪行 - Likely means 'to act as an attendant' or 'to accompany,' but the exact meaning is unclear due to the archaic nature of the term.
  • 前方后圆 - Describes a shape or formation, possibly referring to a military or ceremonial arrangement.
  • 有放挫啼曰 - Literally means 'someone cried out,' but the context suggests it refers to a person making a statement or request.
  • 乃悬死鼠于鸢足 - Literally means 'then hung a dead mouse on the hawk's foot,' which is likely a metaphor for a clever trap or strategy.
  • 联其目 - Means 'to tie its eyes,' referring to the hawk's eyes.
  • 放而钓焉 - Means 'released and used it as a lure,' describing the action of releasing the hawk with the dead mouse as bait.
  • 二雕果击于鸢盘 - Means 'the two eagles indeed struck at the hawk's plate,' describing the eagles attacking the hawk.
  • 狡兔起前 - Means 'a cunning rabbit rose ahead,' describing a rabbit appearing in front.
  • 上举挝击毙之 - Means 'the emperor raised a club and struck it down,' describing the emperor killing the rabbit.
  • 帝称那庚 - Means 'the emperor praised it,' but the exact meaning of '那庚' is unclear.
  • 从臣皆呼万岁 - Means 'all the attendants cried out 'Long live the emperor!'' describing the courtiers' response.
  • 三月三日 - Refers to the third day of the third month, a traditional Chinese festival.
  • 赐侍臣细柳圈 - Means 'gave the attendants willow rings,' describing a gift given to courtiers.
  • 言带之免虿毒 - Means 'said that wearing them would protect against scorpion poison,' describing the purpose of the willow rings.
  • 寒食日 - Refers to the Cold Food Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
  • 赐侍臣帖彩球，绣草宣台 - Means 'gave the attendants colorful balls and embroidered grass platforms,' describing gifts given to courtiers.
  • 立春日 - Refers to the beginning of spring, a traditional Chinese festival.
  • 赐侍臣彩花树 - Means 'gave the attendants colorful flower trees,' describing a gift given to courtiers.
  • 腊日 - Refers to the Laba Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
  • 赐北门学士口脂、蜡脂 - Means 'gave the scholars at the Northern Gate lip balm and wax,' describing gifts given to scholars.
  • 盛以碧镂牙筒 - Means 'placed them in green-carved ivory tubes,' describing the containers for the gifts.
  • 上尝梦曰（一作白）鸟飞，蝙蝠数十逐而堕地 - Means 'the emperor once dreamed that birds flew, and dozens of bats chased and fell to the ground,' describing a dream.
  • 惊觉，召万回僧曰：“大家即是上天时。” - Means 'he woke up in alarm and summoned the monk Wanhuai, saying, 'Everyone is ascending to heaven now,' describing the emperor's reaction to the dream.
  • 翌日而崩 - Means 'the next day he died,' describing the emperor's death.
  • 睿宗尝阅内库 - Means 'Emperor Ruizong once inspected the imperial treasury,' describing an event.
  • 见一鞭，金色，长四尺，数节有虫啮处，状如盘龙 - Means 'saw a whip, golden in color, four feet long, with several sections gnawed by insects, resembling a coiled dragon,' describing the appearance of the whip.
  • 靶上悬牙牌，题象耳皮 - Means 'on the handle was a toothed plaque inscribed with elephant ear skin,' describing the details of the whip.
  • 或言隋宫库旧物也 - Means 'some said it was an old item from the Sui palace treasury,' describing the origin of the whip.
  • 上为冀王时，寝斋壁上蜗迹成“天”字 - Means 'when the emperor was Prince Ji, a snail trail on the wall of his study formed the character 'tian' (heaven),' describing an event.
  • 上惧，遽扫之 - Means 'the emperor was frightened and quickly swept it away,' describing the emperor's reaction.
  • 经数日如初 - Means 'after several days, it returned to its original state,' describing the recurrence of the snail trail.
  • 及即位，雕玉铸黄金为蜗形，分置于释道像前 - Means 'when he ascended the throne, he carved jade and cast gold into the shape of snails, placing them in front of Buddhist and Taoist statues,' describing the emperor's actions after becoming emperor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中宗 - Refers to Emperor Zhongzong of Tang (656-710), who reigned from 705 to 710.
  • 景龙 - The era name of Emperor Zhongzong's reign (707-710).
  • 睿宗 - Refers to Emperor Ruizong of Tang (662-716), who reigned from 710 to 712.
  • 万回僧 - Refers to the monk Wanhuai, known for his prophetic abilities.
  • 三月三日 - The third day of the third month, a traditional Chinese festival associated with spring and renewal.
  • 寒食日 - The Cold Food Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday observed before the Qingming Festival.
  • 立春日 - The beginning of spring, a traditional Chinese festival marking the start of the new year.
  • 腊日 - The Laba Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday celebrated on the eighth day of the twelfth lunar month.
  • 北门学士 - Scholars who served at the Northern Gate of the imperial palace.
  • 隋宫库 - The imperial treasury of the Sui dynasty, a reference to a previous dynasty.
  • 冀王 - The title of the emperor before he became emperor, referring to his position as Prince Ji.
  • 释道像 - Buddhist and Taoist statues, reflecting the religious practices of the time.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 作吐陪行 - The meaning of this phrase is unclear due to the archaic nature of the term.
  • 有放挫啼曰 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to someone making a statement or request.
  • 乃悬死鼠于鸢足 - The metaphorical meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to a clever trap or strategy.
  • 联其目 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to tying the hawk's eyes.
  • 放而钓焉 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to releasing the hawk with the dead mouse as bait.
  • 二雕果击于鸢盘 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the eagles attacking the hawk.
  • 狡兔起前 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to a cunning rabbit appearing in front.
  • 上举挝击毙之 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor killing the rabbit.
  • 帝称那庚 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor praising something.
  • 从臣皆呼万岁 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the courtiers' response to the emperor's actions.
  • 上尝梦曰（一作白）鸟飞，蝙蝠数十逐而堕地 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor's dream.
  • 惊觉，召万回僧曰：“大家即是上天时。” - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor's reaction to the dream.
  • 翌日而崩 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor's death.
  • 睿宗尝阅内库 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to Emperor Ruizong inspecting the imperial treasury.
  • 见一鞭，金色，长四尺，数节有虫啮处，状如盘龙 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the appearance of the whip.
  • 靶上悬牙牌，题象耳皮 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the details of the whip.
  • 或言隋宫库旧物也 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the origin of the whip.
  • 上为冀王时，寝斋壁上蜗迹成“天”字 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to an event involving a snail trail.
  • 上惧，遽扫之 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor's reaction.
  • 经数日如初 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the recurrence of the snail trail.
  • 及即位，雕玉铸黄金为蜗形，分置于释道像前 - The exact meaning of this phrase is unclear, but it likely refers to the emperor's actions after becoming emperor.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 禁中 - Formal term for the inner palace, where the emperor resides.
  • 阿瞒 - Nickname for Cao Cao, a famous warlord and statesman in Chinese history. Used here as a playful or affectionate term for Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 鸦 - Nickname for Emperor Xuanzong, possibly derived from his given name or a characteristic.
  • 寿安公主 - Title of a princess, indicating her status and rank.
  • 曹野那姬 - Name of the princess's mother, a concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 九月而诞 - Refers to the princess being born in the ninth month of the lunar calendar.
  • 不出降 - Means that the princess did not marry, often due to her special status or circumstances.
  • 衣道服 - Refers to wearing religious or ceremonial robes.
  • 主香火 - Means to manage or tend to the burning of incense, a ritual practice.
  • 小字虫娘 - Pet name or nickname for the princess, indicating a close relationship.
  • 师娘 - Term of endearment or respect, possibly used by the emperor to address the princess.
  • 太上皇 - Title for an abdicated emperor, referring to Emperor Xuanzong after he abdicated in favor of his son, Emperor Suzong.
  • 代宗 - Title of Emperor Suzong, the son of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 起居 - Refers to the daily routine or activities of the emperor.
  • 令名 - Means a good reputation or name.
  • 鸦女 - Term of endearment for the princess, likely due to the emperor's nickname.
  • 尚之 - Means to marry or be betrothed to, indicating a formal union.
  • 封寿安焉 - Means to bestow the title of Princess of Life and Peace upon the princess.
  • 天宝末 - Refers to the end of the Tianbao era, a period during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 交趾 - Ancient name for the southern region of Vietnam, which was part of the Tang Dynasty's territory.
  • 龙脑 - Type of aromatic resin, often used in incense and perfumes.
  • 蝉蚕形 - Describes the shape of the dragon brain, resembling a cicada or silkworm.
  • 波斯言 - Refers to the Persian language or the Persian people, indicating the origin of the information.
  • 瑞龙脑 - Precious form of dragon brain, considered a rare and valuable commodity.
  • 上唯赐贵妃十枚 - Means that the emperor gave ten pieces of the precious dragon brain to the concubine.
  • 香气彻十余步 - Describes the strong and far-reaching fragrance of the dragon brain.
  • 夏日尝与亲王棋 - Means that the emperor often played chess with his princes during the summer.
  • 贺怀智 - Name of a musician, likely a court musician.
  • 独弹琵琶 - Means to play the pipa (a type of lute) alone.
  • 立于局前观之 - Means to stand in front of the chessboard and watch the game.
  • 数子将输 - Means that the emperor was about to lose several pieces.
  • 上局 - Means to place the object on the chessboard.
  • 局子乱 - Means that the chess pieces became disordered.
  • 大悦 - Means greatly pleased or delighted.
  • 风吹贵妃领巾于贺怀智巾上 - Means that the wind blew the princess's scarf onto the musician's head.
  • 良久 - Means for a long time.
  • 回身方落 - Means that the scarf fell off after the musician turned his head.
  • 卸幞头 - Means to remove the head covering.
  • 贮于锦囊中 - Means to store it in a brocade bag.
  • 二皇 - Refers to the two emperors, likely Emperor Suzong and Emperor Daizong.
  • 追思贵妃不已 - Means to continually remember and mourn the concubine.
  • 具奏它日事 - Means to report the incident in detail.
  • 发囊 - Means to open the bag.
  • 此瑞龙脑香也 - Means that this is the precious dragon brain fragrance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 玄宗 - Posthumous name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a prominent ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 禁中 - Inner palace, a sacred and restricted area where the emperor lived.
  • 阿瞒 - Nickname for Cao Cao, a famous warlord and statesman, used here as a playful or affectionate term for Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 鸦 - Nickname for Emperor Xuanzong, possibly derived from his given name or a characteristic.
  • 寿安公主 - Title of a princess, indicating her status and rank.
  • 曹野那姬 - Name of the princess's mother, a concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 天宝末 - End of the Tianbao era, a period marked by the An Lushan Rebellion and the decline of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 交趾 - Ancient name for the southern region of Vietnam, which was part of the Tang Dynasty's territory.
  • 龙脑 - Type of aromatic resin, often used in incense and perfumes, highly valued in ancient China.
  • 波斯言 - Refers to the Persian language or the Persian people, indicating the origin of the information.
  • 瑞龙脑 - Precious form of dragon brain, considered a rare and valuable commodity.
  • 贵妃 - Title of a high-ranking concubine, often used for the most favored wife of the emperor.
  • 天宝 - Era name of Emperor Xuanzong, spanning from 742 to 756 AD.
  • 太上皇 - Title for an abdicated emperor, referring to Emperor Xuanzong after he abdicated in favor of his son, Emperor Suzong.
  • 代宗 - Title of Emperor Suzong, the son of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 灵武 - Place name, likely referring to a location where Emperor Suzong was based during the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 苏澄 - Name of a person, likely a noble or official, who was betrothed to the princess.
  • 贺怀智 - Name of a musician, likely a court musician.

Proper Nouns

  • 玄宗 - Posthumous name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
  • 阿瞒 - Nickname for Cao Cao.
  • 鸦 - Nickname for Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 寿安公主 - Title of a princess.
  • 曹野那姬 - Name of the princess's mother.
  • 天宝 - Era name of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 太上皇 - Title for an abdicated emperor.
  • 代宗 - Title of Emperor Suzong.
  • 灵武 - Place name.
  • 苏澄 - Name of a person.
  • 贺怀智 - Name of a musician.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恩寵莫比 - Highly honorific expression meaning unparalleled favor and grace, indicating extreme royal favor.
  • 錫赍无数 - Means innumerable gifts and favors, emphasizing the abundance of imperial rewards.
  • 品目 - Refers to categories or types of gifts.
  • 桑落酒 - A type of wine, likely a specific brand or variety.
  • 阔尾羊窟利 - An unclear term; it may refer to a type of sheep or a specific dish.
  • 马酪 - Refers to mare's milk or a dairy product made from mare's milk.
  • 音声人两部 - Likely refers to two groups of musicians or performers.
  • 野猪鲜 - Refers to fresh wild boar meat.
  • 鲫鱼并手刀子 - Refers to carp and a type of knife, possibly a ceremonial or specialized knife.
  • 清酒 - Refers to clear, unfermented rice wine.
  • 大锦 - Refers to large, fine brocade or silk fabric.
  • 苏造真符宝舆 - An unclear term; it may refer to a special carriage or vehicle.
  • 余甘煎 - Refers to a type of candy or confection made from the fruit of the *Phyllanthus emblica* tree.
  • 辽泽野鸡 - Refers to wild pheasants from the Liaozuo region.
  • 五术汤 - Refers to a type of herbal soup or medicine.
  • 金石凌汤一剂及药童昔贤子就宅煎 - Refers to a medicinal soup made from gold and stone ingredients, possibly prepared by a skilled apothecary.
  • 蒸梨 - Refers to steamed pears.
  • 金平脱犀头匙箸 - Refers to gold-plated ivory chopsticks and spoons.
  • 金银平脱隔馄饨盘 - Refers to a tray for serving dumplings, decorated with gold and silver.
  • 金花狮子瓶 - Refers to a vase shaped like a lion, possibly decorated with gold.
  • 平脱着足叠子 - Refers to a type of mat or cushion.
  • 熟线绫接靴 - Refers to boots made from fine silk.
  • 金大脑盘 - Refers to a large, possibly ornate plate.
  • 银平脱破觚 - Refers to a silver-plated wine vessel.
  • 八角花乌屏风 - Refers to a screen decorated with eight-pointed flowers and black designs.
  • 银凿镂铁锁 - Refers to a silver-plated iron lock.
  • 帖白（一曰花）檀香床 - Refers to a bed made of sandalwood, possibly with white or floral decorations.
  • 绿白平细背席 - Refers to a mat or cushion with green and white patterns.
  • 绣鹅毛毡兼令瑶令光就宅张设 - Refers to embroidered goose feather mats, possibly arranged in the residence.
  • 金鸾紫罗绯罗立马宝 - Refers to a set of items, possibly including a horse, decorated with gold, purple, and red silk.
  • 鸡袍 - Refers to a type of robe or garment.
  • 龙须夹帖 - Refers to a type of paper or document, possibly with dragon motifs.
  • 八斗金渡银酒瓮银瓶平脱掏魁织锦筐 - Refers to a wine jar and bottle, possibly decorated with gold and silver, and a woven basket.
  • 银笊篱 - Refers to a silver strainer or sieve.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 魏使李同轨、陆操聘梁 - Wei sent Li Tonggui and Lu Cao to pay a visit to Liang. The term '聘' (pay a visit) is used in the context of diplomatic missions.
  • 入乐游苑西门内青油幕下 - Enter the western gate of Le You Yuan and go inside the blue oil tent. The phrase '青油幕' (blue oil tent) refers to a tent used for official receptions or meetings.
  • 梁主备三仗，乘舆从南门入 - The Liang ruler prepared three rows of guards, and the imperial carriage entered from the south gate. The term '三仗' (three rows of guards) indicates a high level of ceremonial formality.
  • 操等东面再拜 - Lu Cao and others bowed twice facing east. The phrase '东面再拜' (bowed twice facing east) is a traditional form of greeting in ancient China.
  • 遣书舍人殷灵宣旨慰劳 - Sent the scribe Yin Ling to deliver the ruler's instructions and to comfort and encourage them. The term '书舍人' (scribe) refers to an official responsible for writing and delivering messages.
  • 具有辞答 - They had a formal reply. The phrase '具辞答' (formal reply) indicates a polite and structured response.
  • 其中庭设钟悬及百戏殿上 - In the central courtyard, there were bells hanging and various entertainments on the palace platform. The term '百戏' (various entertainments) refers to a variety of performances, including music, dance, and acrobatics.
  • 流杯池中行酒 - Wine was served in the flowing cup pool. The phrase '流杯池' (flowing cup pool) refers to a pool where cups filled with wine were floated, and guests would take turns drinking from them.
  • 题曰御杯，自余各题官姓之杯 - The cup for the ruler was marked 'Imperial Cup,' and the others were marked with the officials' surnames. The term '御杯' (Imperial Cup) indicates the cup reserved for the ruler.
  • 至前者即饮 - Those who reached it drank from it. The phrase '至前者即饮' (those who reached it drank from it) describes the process of drinking from the flowing cups.
  • 又图象旧事，令随流而转 - There were also images of past events, which were made to flow along with the cups. The term '图象旧事' (images of past events) refers to depictions of historical scenes.
  • 始至讫于坐罢，首尾不绝也 - From the beginning to the end of the gathering, they flowed continuously without interruption. The phrase '首尾不绝' (flowed continuously) emphasizes the continuous nature of the event.

  • 釋氏書言須彌山南面有閻扶樹，月過，樹影入月中 - Buddhist text mentioning a tree named Yanfu on the southern slope of Mount Sumeru, whose shadow falls into the moon as it passes.
  • 或言月中蟾桂地影也，空處水影也，此語差近 - Possible explanation suggesting that the moon's rabbit and cassia tree are shadows of the earth and water, respectively, which is closer to reality.

  • 六天横北道，此是鬼神家 - '六天' (六天) refers to the six heavens, while '横北道' (横北道) might relate to the northern path or direction. '鬼神家' (鬼神家) means 'the home of ghosts and spirits,' referring to the underworld.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 酆都稻名重思 - Fengdu rice named Chong Si, where '重思' is a name for a type of rice.
  • 其米如石榴子，粒稍大，味如菱 - Describes the appearance and taste of the rice, using metaphors: 'its grains are like pomegranate seeds, slightly larger, and taste like water caltrops.'
  • 霏霏春暮，翠矣重思 - 'Faintly at the end of spring, the green Chong Si rice is already flourishing,' a poetic description of the rice growing in late spring.
  • 云气交被，嘉应时 - 'Clouds and mist intermingle, and the good rice responds to the season,' a metaphorical description of favorable weather conditions for the rice.
  • 夏启为东明公，文王为西明公，邵公为南明公，季札为北明公 - Refers to historical figures (Yu the Great, King Wen of Zhou, Duke of Shao, and Ji Zha) who are deified as guardians of the four directions in the underworld.
  • 四时主四方鬼 - 'They govern the spirits of the four directions during the four seasons,' indicating their role as overseers of the underworld.
  • 至忠至孝之人，命终皆为地下主者 - 'Those who are extremely loyal and filial, when they die, all become rulers of the underworld,' a belief in the afterlife reward for virtue.
  • 一百四十年乃授下仙之教，授以大道 - 'After one hundred and forty years, they are taught the teachings of lower immortals and given the great Dao,' referring to the path of spiritual cultivation.
  • 有上圣之德，命终受三官书，为地下主者 - 'Those with the virtue of a supreme sage, upon death, receive the books of the Three Officials and become rulers of the underworld,' indicating a higher spiritual rank.
  • 一千年乃转三官之五帝，复一千四百年方得游行太清，为九宫之中仙 - 'After one thousand years, they transform into the Five Emperors of the Three Officials, and after another one thousand four hundred years, they can travel freely in the Great Clarity and become central immortals in the Nine Palaces,' describing a long spiritual journey.
  • 又有为善爽鬼者，三官清鬼者，或先世有功，在三官流 - 'There are also righteous ghosts, pure ghosts of the Three Officials, or those who had merit in previous lives and are in the stream of the Three Officials,' referring to different types of spirits.
  • 逮后嗣易世练化，改世更生 - 'When their descendants change and undergo transformation, they are reborn in a new life,' indicating reincarnation.
  • 此七世阴德，根叶相及也 - 'This is the accumulated virtue of seven generations, where roots and leaves are connected,' emphasizing the continuity of virtue across generations.
  • 命终当道遗脚一骨，以归三官，余骨随身而迁 - 'When they die, they leave behind one bone on the path to return to the Three Officials, and the rest of their bones follow them,' a description of the afterlife process.
  • 男左女右，皆受书为地下主者，二百八十年，乃得进处地仙之道矣 - 'Men go left and women go right, all receiving books to become rulers of the underworld, and after two hundred and eighty years, they can advance to the path of terrestrial immortals,' describing the spiritual progression.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 酆都 - 'Fengdu' is a mythical place in Chinese folklore, often associated with the underworld.
  • 夏启 - 'Yu the Great,' a legendary Chinese ruler and the founder of the Xia Dynasty.
  • 文王 - 'King Wen of Zhou,' a revered figure in Chinese history and a key ancestor of the Zhou Dynasty.
  • 邵公 - 'Duke of Shao,' a historical figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 季札 - 'Ji Zha,' a nobleman of the State of Wu during the Spring and Autumn period, known for his integrity and wisdom.
  • 三官 - 'The Three Officials,' deities in Chinese folk religion who oversee various aspects of the afterlife.
  • 五帝 - 'The Five Emperors,' a group of legendary rulers in Chinese mythology.
  • 太清 - 'The Great Clarity,' a term in Daoism referring to a high spiritual realm.
  • 九宫 - 'The Nine Palaces,' a concept in Chinese cosmology and Daoism, often associated with the heavens and the afterlife.
  • 三官流 - 'The stream of the Three Officials,' a metaphor for the spiritual lineage or path associated with the Three Officials.
  • 地仙 - 'Terrestrial Immortals,' a type of immortal in Chinese mythology who have achieved a high level of spiritual cultivation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 老君西越流沙，历八十一国 - Literal description of Laozi's journey westward across the desert, passing through eighty-one countries.
  • 乌弋、身毒为浮屠，化被三千国 - Refers to the spread of Buddhism in the regions of Uddiyana and Gandhara, which had a significant influence over three thousand kingdoms.
  • 九万品戒经 - Reference to the extensive Buddhist scriptures, specifically the 'Nine Ten Thousand Precepts Sutra'.
  • 汉所获大月支《复位经》是也 - Indicates that the 'Recitation Sutra' (复位经) obtained by the Han dynasty from the Great Yuechi is a specific example of these scriptures.
  • 孔子为元宫仙，佛为三十三天仙 - Metaphorical comparison between Confucius and the Buddha, suggesting their spiritual attainments and positions in the heavens.
  • 延宾官主所为道，在竺干 - Reference to the teachings or practices of the 'Guest Official Master' in India, implying the transmission of Buddhist doctrines.
  • 有古先生，善入无为 - Refers to ancient sages who excel in achieving a state of non-action (wu wei).
  • 《释老志》亦曰佛于西域得道 - Reference to the 'Biographies of the Laozi and Buddha' (释老志), which states that the Buddha attained enlightenment in the Western Regions.
  • 陶胜力言，小方诸国多奉佛，不死，服五笙精，读《夏归藏》，用之以飞行也 - Statement by Tao Sheng regarding the practice of immortality in certain countries, involving the consumption of 'five bamboo spirits' and reading the 'Summer Hidden' (夏归藏) to achieve flight.
  • 藏经，菩萨戒也 - Reference to the scriptures as containing the precepts for bodhisattvas.
  • 方诸山在乙地 - Geographical reference to the 'Fangzhu Mountain' located in a specific place (乙地).
  • 太极真仙中，庄周为闱编郎 - Metaphorical statement about Zhuangzi's position among the true immortals in the Taiji realm.
  • 八十一戒，千二百善，入洞天 - Refers to the eighty-one precepts and twelve hundred virtues that lead to entering the 'Hollow Heaven' (洞天).
  • 二百三十戒，二千善，登山上灵官 - Refers to the two hundred thirty precepts and two thousand virtues that lead to ascending to the 'Mountain Spirit Official' (山上灵官).
  • 万善，升玉清 - Refers to the attainment of ten thousand virtues leading to ascending to the 'Jade Clarity' (玉清) realm.
  • 白志见腹，名在琼简者；目有绿筋，名在金赤书者；阴有伏骨，名在琳札青书者；胸有偃骨，名在星书者；眼四规，名在方诸者；掌理回菌，名在绿籍者 - Describes various physical marks and their corresponding records in different celestial registers.
  • 有前相，皆上仙也，可不学，其道自至 - Statement about those with certain auspicious signs being naturally destined to become immortals, without the need for formal learning.
  • 其次鼻有玄山，腹有玄丘，亦仙相也 - Refers to other auspicious signs such as a 'black mountain' on the nose and a 'black hill' in the abdomen, indicating potential for immortality.
  • 或口气不洁，性耐秽，则坏玄丘之相矣 - Warning that impure breath or a tendency to tolerate filth can ruin the auspicious signs associated with immortality.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 五藏、九宫、十二室、四支、五体、三焦、九窍、百八十机关、三百六十骨节 - Anatomical terms in Classical Chinese, referring to the body's internal organs, energy centers, and structural components. Requires precise translation.
  • 三魂可拘，七魄可制 - "三魂" (three souls) and "七魄" (seven spirits) are concepts from Chinese metaphysics, representing the spiritual essence of a person. "可拘" and "可制" imply that these can be restrained or controlled.
  • 庚申日，伏尸言人过 - "庚申日" is a specific day in the Chinese calendar, often associated with spiritual rituals. "伏尸言人过" refers to the belief that spirits on this day report human misdeeds.
  • 本命日，天曹计人行 - "本命日" is one's birth day, and "天曹计人行" suggests that on this day, celestial beings record one's actions.
  • 三尸一日三朝 - "三尸" are evil spirits believed to reside in the human body. "一日三朝" means they report to the heavens three times a day.
  • 魂以精为根，魄以目为户 - "魂" (soul) is rooted in essence, and "魄" (spirit) enters through the eyes, reflecting the relationship between the spiritual and physical aspects of the body.
  • 七守庚申三尸灭，三守庚申三尸伏 - Practices involving the number seven and three, believed to control or eliminate the "三尸" spirits.
  • 仙药 - Refers to elixirs of immortality, a common theme in Taoist and alchemical texts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五藏、九宫、十二室、四支、五体、三焦、九窍、百八十机关、三百六十骨节 - These terms are rooted in traditional Chinese medicine and metaphysics, reflecting the holistic view of the body as an interconnected system.
  • 三尸 - The belief in "三尸" spirits is a part of Chinese folk religion and Taoist alchemy, where they are seen as sources of human corruption.
  • 仙药 - The list of elixirs reflects the pursuit of immortality in Taoist and alchemical traditions, with each ingredient symbolizing a different aspect of spiritual or physical transformation.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence "五藏、九宫、十二室、四支、五体、三焦、九窍、百八十机关、三百六十骨节，三万六千神随其所而居之" is long and lists body parts and spiritual entities, requiring careful parsing to maintain the relationship between the physical and spiritual aspects.
  • The sentence "魂以精为根，魄以目为户" is elliptical, with "魂" and "魄" serving as subjects for the predicates "以精为根" and "以目为户," respectively.
  • The sentence "三守庚申三尸伏" uses the number three for emphasis, but the exact meaning of "伏" (to subdue) requires contextual interpretation.

  • 本命日 - One's birth day, significant in Chinese spiritual practices.

  • 剖左掖而生，生而白首 - Describes Laozi being born from his mother's left armpit and being born with white hair.
  • 又曰青帝劫末，元气改运，托形于洪氏之胞 - Another version of Laozi's conception, where he was born at the end of the Green Emperor's era when the primordial qi changed, and he took form in the womb of the Hong family.
  • 又曰李母本元君也，日精入口，吞而有孕 - Another version of Laozi's conception, where his mother, originally a celestial being, swallowed the essence of the sun and became pregnant.

  • 白日去曰上解 - Describes a type of *shi jie* that occurs during the day, symbolizing a higher or more exalted form of transcendence.
  • 夜半去曰下解 - Describes a type of *shi jie* that occurs at midnight, symbolizing a lower or more earthly form of transcendence.
  • 向晓、向暮谓之地下主者 - Refers to *shi jie* occurring just before dawn or dusk, and associates this timing with the underworld or the realm of the dead.
  • 太一守尸 - Daoist concept where *Tai Yi* (太一), a supreme deity, guards the corpse during the *shi jie* process.
  • 三魂营骨 - Refers to the three souls (hun) guarding the bones.
  • 七魄卫肉 - Refers to the seven spirits (po) guarding the flesh.

  • 赵成子后五六年 - Refers to Zhao Chengzi (赵成子), a historical figure, and the time period following his death.
  • 肉朽骨在 - Indicates that the flesh has decayed but the bones remain intact.
  • 液血于内 - Means that blood remains inside the body.
  • 紫色发外 - Refers to a purple color appearing on the surface of the body, possibly due to blood or other bodily fluids.
  • 若人暂死 - Means 'if a person temporarily dies,' referring to a brief state of death or near-death.
  • 适太阴权过三官 - Refers to the soul passing through the underworld or the realm of the dead, governed by the Three Officials (三官).
  • 血沉脉散 - Means that the blood settles and the pulse disperses, indicating the cessation of life.
  • 而五藏自生 - Refers to the internal organs seemingly regenerating or continuing to function.
  • 白骨如玉 - Describes the bones becoming as white and smooth as jade, a sign of purity or refinement.
  • 三光惟息 - Refers to the three lights (sun, moon, and stars) resting or ceasing, symbolizing the end of life.
  • 太神内闭 - Means that the great spirit (or soul) is internally sealed or contained.
  • 或三年至三十年 - Indicates a range of time, from three to thirty years, during which the *shi jie* process may occur.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '白日尸解自是仙，非尸解也' is ambiguous, suggesting that while it appears to be a form of immortality, it is not a true transformation.
  • The clauses '鹿皮公吞玉华而流虫出尸，王西城漱龙胎而死诀，饮琼精而扣棺' are parallel but can be read as independent statements, each describing a different method of achieving immortality.
  • The clauses '仇季子咽金液而臭彻百里，季主服霜散以潜升，而头足异处' are also parallel, each describing a different transformation process.
  • The clauses '黑狄咽虹丹而投水，宁生服石脑而赴火，柏成纳气而胃肠三腐' are parallel, each describing a different dramatic act of transformation.
  • The sentence '句曲山五芝，求之者投金环二双于石间，勿顾念，必得矣' is elliptical, requiring the reader to infer the steps needed to obtain the mushrooms.
  • The sentence '真人用宝剑以尸解者，蝉化之上品也' is elliptical, requiring the reader to understand the context of the magical sword and the transformation.
  • The sentence '锻用七月庚申、八月辛酉日' is elliptical, requiring the reader to understand the significance of the specific days.
  • The sentence '长三尺九寸，广一寸四分，厚三分半，杪九寸' is a list of measurements, which must be read as a single unit.
  • The sentence '青乌公入华山，四百七十一岁，十二试三不过' is elliptical, requiring the reader to understand the context of the tests and the partial achievement of immortality.
  • The sentence '有传先生入然山七年，老君与之木钻，使穿一盘石，石厚五尺，曰：“此石穴，当得道。”积四十七年，石穿，得神丹' is elliptical, requiring the reader to understand the symbolic meaning of the stone and the divine elixir.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 龟兹板 - A type of wood from the Kucha region, known for its unique properties.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The elliptical question '言太山老君师，颇记无？' can be interpreted as 'Do you remember much or nothing about the Master of Mount Tai?'
  • The fortune-telling prediction '若来由东南，止西北，禄命卒矣' requires context to fully understand.
  • The description '降魄之处，非馆非寺，非途非署' of a mysterious location requires context to fully understand.
  • The description '病起于鱼飧，休于龟兹板' of illness and remedy requires context to fully understand.
  • The sequence of events '适雇工治木，房怪其木理成形，问之，道士称：“数月前，有贾客施数段龟兹板，今治为屠苏也。”' requires context to fully understand.

  • 龟兹板 - Kucha board, a type of wood from the Kucha region.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 长六尺 - A unit of measurement; one chi is roughly 20–23 cm, so this is about 1.2–1.4 meters.
  • 手大尺余 - Implies large hands, a physical characteristic.
  • 每揖人，手过胸前 - A specific gesture, possibly symbolic or ritualistic.

  • 晚年往往言将来事 - Indicates prophetic abilities.

  • 人不之悟 - Indicates the prophecy was not immediately recognized.
  • 火焚数百家 - A disaster that happened as a result of the prophecy.
  • 八人乃火字也 - The Chinese character for "fire" (火) is composed of eight strokes, or "eight people."
  • 每入山，虎群随之 - A sign of his supernatural power or connection with nature.
  • 曾于江岸与弟子数十玩月 - A poetic or mystical scene.

  • 弟子中两人见月规半天，楼殿金阙满焉 - A vision of a celestial or spiritual realm.
  • 数息间，不复见 - The vision disappears quickly, suggesting it was ephemeral or supernatural.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Wang Jiao (王皎) and Zhai Tian Shi (翟天师) are mystics or Daoist sages with prophetic abilities.
  • Tianbao period (天宝) was the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (r. 712–756), a time of prosperity that ended with the An Lushan Rebellion, a major event in Chinese history.
  • An Lushan and Shi Siming (安、史) were the leaders of the rebellion, which severely weakened the Tang Dynasty.
  • "天师" (Tianshi) is a title used for Daoist masters, particularly those associated with the Celestial Masters (天师道) tradition.
  • "火" (fire) as a character with eight strokes is a linguistic and symbolic play, common in classical Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 畋 - Another name for Wang Jiao.
  • 达奚侍郎 - A high-ranking official.
  • 翟天师 - A Daoist sage.
  • 安、史 - Refers to An Lushan and Shi Siming, the rebel leaders.
  • 夔州市 - A location in Sichuan, near the Yangtze River.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乃斫一枯桑树，成数筐札，聚于盘上 - A sequence of actions: chopping down a dry mulberry tree, splitting it into strips, and gathering them on a tray. Focuses on transforming raw material into a usable form.
  • 哄之悉成牛肉 - Magical or transformative phrase where the strips of wood are magically turned into beef. Cannot be directly translated.
  • 复汲数瓶水，顷之乃旨酒也 - Another magical transformation where water is turned into fine wine. '顷之' indicates a short time, suggesting quick transformation.
  • 村老皆醉饱，获束缣三千 - Describes villagers becoming drunk and full, and receiving a large sum of money (三千束缣). Focuses on immediate satisfaction and reward.
  • 秀才方渐，谢雇者曰 - Somewhat ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the scholar gradually' or 'the scholar, in turn.' Context suggests the scholar is responding to the employer.
  • 予固异人，有少失，谪于下贱，合役于秀才 - '予固异人' indicates the employer is a special or different person. Explains that the employer has been demoted to a lowly position and is now serving the scholar.
  • 若限未足，复须力于它人 - Conditional statement suggesting that if the time limit is not enough, the employer will need to seek help from others.
  • 请秀才勿变常，庶卒某事也 - Polite request to the scholar not to change his usual behavior. '庶卒某事也' suggests ensuring the completion of a task.
  • 秀才虽诺之，每呼指，色上面，蹙蹙不安 - Describes the scholar's reaction to the employer's requests. '色上面' and '蹙蹙不安' indicate that the scholar looks uncomfortable and anxious.
  • 雇者乃辞曰 - Indicates that the employer then leaves, saying something.
  • 因说秀才修短穷达之数 - Introduces a discussion of fate or destiny, including both good and bad fortune.
  • 唯淤泥中朱漆筋及发，药力不能化 - Describes a limitation of the magical transformation, stating that only certain things, like red lacquered ropes and hair, cannot be transformed by the magic.
  • 因去，不知所之也 - Concludes the employer's departure, indicating that his destination is unknown.

  • 法自三身、五位、四果、七支至十八界、三十七道品等 - Refers to various Buddhist concepts such as the three bodies, five positions, four fruits, seven limbs, eighteen elements, and thirty-seven factors of enlightenment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 释门 - Refers to the Buddhist tradition.
  • 三界 - The three realms in Buddhist cosmology: the desire realm, the form realm, and the formless realm.
  • 二十八天 - The twenty-eight heavens in the Buddhist cosmology.
  • 四洲 - The four continents in Buddhist cosmology: Jambudvipa, Uttarakuru, Pūrvavideha, and Aparagodānīya.
  • 华藏世界 - The Pure Land of the Lotus Sutra, a realm of perfect purity and enlightenment.
  • 八寒八热地狱 - The eight cold and eight hot hells, places of suffering in Buddhist cosmology.
  • 三身 - The three bodies of the Buddha: the Dharmakaya, the Sambhogakaya, and the Nirmanakaya.
  • 五位 - The five positions in the Tiantai school of Buddhism: the position of the Buddha, the position of the Dharma, the position of the Sangha, the position of the precepts, and the position of wisdom.
  • 四果 - The four fruits of enlightenment in Theravada Buddhism: the fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returner, the fruit of non-returner, and the fruit of arhat.
  • 七支 - The seven limbs of meditation in Zen Buddhism: the seven factors of enlightenment.
  • 十八界 - The eighteen elements in Buddhist philosophy: six sense organs, six sense objects, and six consciousnesses.
  • 三十七道品 - The thirty-seven factors of enlightenment in Buddhism, including the four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts, the four bases of power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of enlightenment, and the eightfold path.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long phrase '释门三界二十八天、四洲至华藏世界、八寒八热地狱等' lists various elements of Buddhist cosmology and must be read as a single unit.
  • The phrase '法自三身、五位、四果、七支至十八界、三十七道品等' lists various Buddhist concepts and must be read as a single unit.
  • The clause '入释者率能言之' states that those who enter the Buddhist path generally can speak of these things.
  • The clause '今不复具，录其事尤异者' indicates that the author will not list all the details but will focus on the particularly unusual ones.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '色界天下石，经十万八千三百八十三年方至地' describes the journey of a stone from the Desire Realm to the earth, using hyperbolic numbers to emphasize the vastness of time.
  • The sentence '阎浮提人生三肘半至四肘，骨四（一曰五）十五，脉十三' lists the physical characteristics of people in Jambudvīpa, including their height, number of bones, and number of major arteries or veins.
  • The sentence '身虫有毛灯真血，禅都摩虫流行血中' describes two types of worms or parasites that live in the human body, one associated with blood and the other circulating in the blood.
  • The sentence '善色虫处粪中，令人安乐，起根虫饱则喜' describes two types of worms, one that brings comfort by living in feces and another that becomes happy when full.
  • The sentence '欢喜□虫能见众梦，又有癀痪等' describes a worm that can see dreams and mentions other types of ailments or diseases.
  • The sentence '赊婆罗人穿唇，驼面目有诸人，二足，师子有翼，女人狗面' describes various people and creatures, including a people who pierce their lips, a person with a hump on their face, a lion with wings, and a woman with a dog-like face.
  • The sentence '有林名吱多迦，罗刹所住，旬目问行百千由旬' describes a forest inhabited by demons and a journey of immense distance.
  • The sentence '洲有赤地黑玉铜康白等，郁单越鸡多迦等天河七十' describes a continent with various types of land and mentions seventy rivers in the Uttarakuru continent.
  • The sentence '自在无畏四天王否如鸭音林、麒麟陀树、迦吱多那等，二十五鹿名' mentions the Four Heavenly Kings and various trees and deer.
  • The sentence '有山多牛头旃檀，天人与阿修罗斗，伤者于此涂香，提罗迦树花，见日光即开' describes a mountain with sandalwood, a battle between gods and demons, and a tree whose flowers open in sunlight.

  • 土地唯有象牙及阿末香 - Description of the land: 'the land has only ivory and ambergris.'

  • 大食频讨袭之 - Description of military action: 'the Arabs frequently attacked it.'
  • 昆吾国，累堑为丘，象浮屠，有三层，尸干居上，尸湿居下，以近葬为至孝 - Description of the kingdom of Kunwu: 'in the kingdom of Kunwu, trenches are piled up to form hills, like stupas, with three stories, the dry corpses are placed above, the wet corpses are placed below, considering near burial as the utmost filial piety.'

  • 婆罗遮，并服狗头猴面，男女无昼夜歌舞 - Description of the kingdom of Pala: 'in the kingdom of Pala, they wear dog heads and monkey faces, men and women dance day and night.'

  • 取大莲叶置砚格上 - Means 'they took large lotus leaves and placed them on the inkstone tray.'
  • 盛酒二升 - Means 'they filled them with two liters of wine.'
  • 以簪刺叶，令与柄通 - Describes the process of piercing the lotus leaf with a hairpin to create a passage to the stem.
  • 屈茎上轮菌如象鼻 - Means 'the stem was bent to form a spiral shape like an elephant's trunk.'
  • 传吸之，名为碧杯 - Describes the method of passing the wine through the stem and naming it 'Green Lotus Cup.'
  • 历下学之 - Means 'people in Lixia learned this method.'
  • 言酒味杂莲气，香冷胜于水 - Describes the wine's flavor as mixed with the fragrance of lotus, and its cold, fragrant taste surpassing that of water.

  • 核大如六升瓠 - Describes the nut as large as a six-liter gourd.
  • 注水其中，俄顷水成酒 - Means 'when water was poured into it, it turned into wine in a short time.'
  • 一名青田壶，亦曰青田酒 - Refers to the same item as 'Qingtian Pot' or 'Qingtian Wine.'
  • 蜀后主有桃核两扇 - Refers to the two peach pits owned by the Later Shu emperor.
  • 每扇着仁处，约盛水五升 - Means 'each pit could hold about five liters of water.'
  • 良久水成酒味醉人 - Describes the water turning into wine after a long time, intoxicating the drinker.
  • 更互贮水，以供其宴 - Means 'they alternated storing water in the pits to provide for their feasts.'
  • 即不知得自何处 - Means 'it is unknown where they obtained the pits.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 粽（一曰糁）熬将军、油蒸扌交尉、霍州刺史 - Titles of military and administrative positions, possibly with symbolic or metaphorical meanings.
  • 脯腊如故 - Means 'as before' or 'unchanged,' likely referring to the continuation of previous duties or status.
  • 肃承将命，含灰屏息 - A formal expression meaning 'to respectfully accept the command and remain silent,' emphasizing obedience and humility.
  • 凭笼临鼎，载兢载惕 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to stand by the pot and be cautious,' symbolizing vigilance and carefulness in one's duties.
  • 美愧夏鳝，味惭冬鲤 - A metaphorical expression comparing oneself to 'summer eels' (likely a reference to something undesirable) and 'winter carp' (likely a reference to something desirable), expressing self-deprecation.
  • 常怀鲐服之诮，每惧鳖岩之讥 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to always bear the ridicule of old age and fear the criticism of being out of place,' expressing self-doubt and anxiety.
  • 漱流湖底，枕石泥中 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to rinse at the bottom of the lake and rest on stones and mud,' symbolizing humility and simplicity.
  • 高赏殊私，曲蒙钩拔 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to receive special favor and be lifted up,' expressing gratitude for unexpected kindness.
  • 超升绮席，忝预玉盘 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to rise above the embroidered seat and be unworthy to be included in the jade dish,' expressing humility and gratitude for being honored.
  • 远厕玳筵，猥颁象箸 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to be far from the precious banquet and receive the ivory chopsticks,' expressing humility and gratitude for being honored.
  • 泽覃紫篝，恩加黄腹 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to be blessed by the purple tent and have mercy extended to the yellow belly,' expressing gratitude for receiving benefits.
  • 鸣姜动椒，纡苏佩傥 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to have ginger and pepper stir and the fragrance of scallions and garlic,' symbolizing the preparation of a feast.
  • 轻瓢才动，则枢盘如烟；浓汁暂停，则兰肴成列 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'when the light ladle is just moved, the main dish is like smoke; when the thick broth is paused, the fragrant dishes are arranged in rows,' describing the preparation and serving of a feast.
  • 宛转绿齑之中，逍遥朱唇之内 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to waver in the green paste and roam in the red lips,' symbolizing the enjoyment of food.
  • 御恩噬泽，九殒弗辞 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to repay the imperial grace and be willing to die nine times,' expressing deep gratitude and loyalty.
  • 不任屏营之诚，谨列铜枪门，奉表以闻 - A formal expression meaning 'unable to bear the sincerity of being in a state of anxiety, I respectfully list the copper spear gate and present this memorial,' expressing the act of submitting a formal document.
  • 省表具知，卿池沼缙绅，陂渠俊，穿蒲入荇，肥滑有闻，允堪兹选，无劳谢也 - A formal expression meaning 'after reviewing the memorial, I know that you, like a gentleman in the pond, are among the talented and refined, and are well-known for your skills and virtues, truly deserving of this selection, no need to thank,' expressing approval and recognition.

  • 少从军 - Literal phrase meaning 'young and serving in the army,' used to describe someone who joined the military at a young age.
  • 善驴鞠 - Specialized term indicating proficiency in a game or sport involving a ball and a donkey, possibly a form of polo or another ancient game.
  • 豆脱杖捷如胶焉 - Obscure phrase describing the agility and skill of the person in using a stick (possibly a bat or a similar implement) with a ball, suggesting a level of expertise akin to being glued to the action.
  • 后为黔南观察使 - Straightforward statement indicating that the person later became an observer or inspector in the region of Qian Nan.
  • 少，遍身刺一蛇 - Describes a young person who had a snake tattooed all over their body, starting from the right hand.
  • 口张臂食两指 - Vivid description of the snake tattoo, with its mouth open and arms reaching out to encircle two fingers.
  • 绕腕匝颈 - Indicates that the snake wraps around the wrist and neck.
  • 龃龉在腹 - Suggests that the snake's teeth or jaw are inside the abdomen.
  • 拖股而尾及焉 - Describes the snake's tail dragging down to the buttocks.
  • 对宾侣常衣覆其手 - The person would often cover their hands with clothing when in the presence of guests.

  • 捉优伶辈曰：“蛇咬尔。” - They would grab actors and say, 'The snake is biting you.'
  • 优伶等即大叫毁而为痛状 - The actors would then pretend to be in pain and cry out.
  • 以此为戏乐 - This behavior was used for entertainment.
  • 宝历中，长乐里门有百姓刺臂 - During the Bao Li era, a commoner in Chang Le Li gate had a tattoo on their arm.
  • 数十人环瞩之 - Several dozen people gathered around to watch.
  • 忽有一人，白衤阑屠苏 - Suddenly, a person appeared wearing a white robe and a hat, smiling and looking around.
  • 顷首微笑而去 - They nodded and smiled before leaving.
  • 未十步，百姓子刺血如衄 - Within ten steps, the commoner's son experienced bleeding as if from a nosebleed.
  • 痛若次骨 - The pain was intense, almost as if it were in the bones.
  • 俄顷出血斗余 - Soon, the bleeding amounted to more than a dou (斗) of blood.
  • 众人疑向观者 - The crowd suspected the person who had just left.
  • 令其父从而求之 - They told the father to go after the person.
  • 其人不承 - The person denied it.
  • 其父拜数十 - The father bowed several times.
  • 乃捻撮土若祝 - The father then picked up some soil and made as if to offer a prayer.
  • 如其言，血止 - As the father spoke, the bleeding stopped.
  • 成式三从兄遘，贞元中，尝过黄坑 - During the Zhenyuan era, Cheng Shi's third cousin, Gou, once passed through Huang Keng.
  • 有从者拾髑颅骨数片 - A follower picked up several pieces of a skull.
  • 将为药 - They intended to use the bones as medicine.
  • 一片上有“逃走奴”三字，痕如淡墨 - One piece had the words 'escaped slave' written in faint ink.
  • 方知黥踪入骨也 - This revealed that the tattoo had been deeply embedded in the skin.
  • 从者夜梦一人，掩面从其索骨曰 - The follower dreamed of a person who covered their face and asked for the bones.
  • “我羞甚，幸君为我深藏之，当福君。” - The person said, 'I am very ashamed. Please bury the bones for me, and I will bless you.'
  • 从者惊觉毛戴，遽为埋之 - The follower woke up in shock and immediately buried the bones.
  • 后有事，鬼仿佛梦中报之 - Later, when something happened, the ghost seemed to report it in a dream.
  • 以是获财，欲至十万而卒 - Because of this, the follower gained wealth, almost reaching ten thousand, and then died.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 成式至德坊三从伯父 - Refers to a specific familial relationship, 'Chengshi, the third uncle from the Zhidefang area.'
  • 少时于阳羡家 - 'When he was young, at the house in Yangxian.'
  • 乃亲故也 - 'He was a close relative,' where '亲故' means 'close kin' or 'near relative.'
  • 夜遇雷雨 - 'Encountered thunder and rain at night,' a straightforward description of a natural event.
  • 每电起，光中见有人头数十，大如栲栳 - 'Every time lightning struck, in the light, he saw dozens of heads, as large as a winnowing basket,' where '栲栳' (kǎolǎo) is a traditional winnowing basket, used here to describe the size of the heads.
  • 柳公权侍郎尝见亲故说 - 'Liu Gongquan, the Attendant, once heard a close relative say,' where '侍郎' (shìláng) is a high-ranking official.
  • 元和末，止建州山寺中 - 'At the end of the Yuanhe era, he stayed in a mountain temple in Jianzhou,' where '元和' (Yuánhé) is a reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, and '止' (zhǐ) means 'to stay' or 'to remain.'
  • 夜中，觉门外喧闹 - 'At night, he heard noise outside the door,' where '觉' (jué) means 'to hear' or 'to sense.'
  • 因潜于窗棂中观之 - 'So he secretly looked out through the window,' where '潜' (qián) means 'to hide' or 'to spy.'
  • 见数人运斤造雷车，如图画者 - 'He saw several people chopping wood to make a thunder chariot, as depicted in pictures,' where '运斤' (yùn jīn) means 'to chop wood,' and '雷车' (léi chē) is a mythological thunder chariot.
  • 久之，一嚏气，忽斗暗，其人两目遂昏焉 - 'After a while, one sneezed, and suddenly everything went dark; the people's eyes became dim,' where '一嚏气' (yī tì qì) means 'one sneeze,' and '斗暗' (dòu àn) means 'suddenly dark.'
  • 处士周洪言 - 'Scholar Zhou Hong said,' where '处士' (chǔshì) means 'a reclusive scholar.'
  • 宝历中，邑客十余人，逃暑会饮 - 'During the Baoli era, more than ten guests from the town gathered to escape the heat and drink,' where '宝历' (Bǎolì) is a reign title of Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
  • 忽暴风雨 - 'Suddenly, a violent storm came,' where '暴风雨' (bàofēngyǔ) means 'violent storm.'
  • 有物坠如ㄑ，两目ㄦㄦ - 'An object fell like a ㄑ, with two eyes ㄦㄦ,' where 'ㄑ' and 'ㄦㄦ' are likely phonetic symbols or placeholders for specific terms.
  • 众人惊伏床下 - 'Everyone was startled and hid under the bed,' where '惊伏' (jīng fú) means 'to be startled and cower.'
  • 倏忽上阶，历视众人，俄失所在 - 'Suddenly, it climbed the stairs, looked at everyone, and then disappeared,' where '倏忽' (shūhū) means 'suddenly,' and '历视' (lì shì) means 'to look at one by one.'
  • 及雨定，稍稍能起，相顾，耳悉泥矣 - 'When the rain stopped, they slowly got up, looked at each other, and found their ears covered in mud,' where '稍稍' (shāoshāo) means 'slowly' or 'gradually.'
  • 邑人言，向来雷震，牛战鸟坠 - 'The townspeople said, the previous lightning strike caused the oxen to fight and the birds to fall,' where '向来' (xiànglái) means 'previously' or 'earlier.'
  • 邑客但觉殷殷而已 - 'The guests from the town only felt a rumbling sound,' where '殷殷' (yīnyīn) means 'rumbling' or 'deep sound.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蜀醫昝殷言 - A formal introduction of the speaker, a physician from Shu (Sichuan), named Zan Yin.
  • 藏气阴多则数梦，阳壮则少梦 - A medical statement about the relationship between internal organs (藏气) and dreams, where an excess of yin leads to frequent dreams, and a strong yang leads to fewer dreams.
  • 梦亦不复记 - Indicates that dreams are not remembered, emphasizing the fleeting nature of dreams.
  • 《周礼》有掌占梦 - Refers to the *Zhouli* (Rites of Zhou), a classic text that mentions the role of a dream interpreter.
  • 以日月星辰各占六梦 - A method of dream interpretation based on the positions of the sun, moon, and stars, which are associated with six types of dreams.
  • 舍萌于四方，以赠恶梦 - A ritual where evil dreams are sent to the four directions, symbolizing the expulsion of bad luck.
  • 汉仪，大傩亻辰子辞，有伯奇食梦 - Refers to a Han dynasty ritual involving the expulsion of evil spirits, with a specific mention of a figure named Bo Qi who eats dreams.
  • 道门言梦者魄妖，或谓三尸所为 - A Daoist perspective that dreams are caused by the soul (魄) or the three worms (三尸), which are believed to cause mischief.
  • 释门言有四：一善恶种子，二四大偏增，三贤圣加持，四善恶征祥 - A Buddhist explanation of dreams, categorizing them into four types: 1) seeds of good and evil actions, 2) imbalances in the four elements, 3) blessings from saints, and 4) omens of good and evil.
  • 成式尝见僧首素言之，言出《藏经》，亦未暇寻讨 - A personal anecdote about the author hearing a monk named Sushu explain these concepts, noting that the information comes from the Buddhist scriptures but has not been thoroughly investigated.
  • 又言梦不可取，取则着，着则怪入 - Another Buddhist teaching that dreams should not be grasped, as doing so can lead to attachment and the entry of strange phenomena.
  • 夫瞽者无梦，则知梦者习也 - A philosophical statement that blind people do not dream, indicating that dreaming is a learned behavior.
  • 成式表兄卢有则，梦看击鼓。及觉，小弟戏叩门为街鼓也 - A personal anecdote about the author's cousin having a dream about watching a drum, which turns out to be a real event when he wakes up.
  • 又成式姑婿裴元裕言，群从中有悦邻女者，梦女遗二樱桃，食之。及觉，核坠枕侧 - Another personal anecdote about the author's brother-in-law's nephew dreaming of a neighbor's daughter giving him two cherries, which he eats, and finding the pits next to his pillow upon waking.
  • 李铉着《李子正辩》，言至精之梦，则梦中身人可见。如刘幽求见妻，梦中身也，则知梦不可以一事推矣 - A reference to a book by Li Xuan, which discusses the nature of very vivid dreams, where the dreamer can see their own body and others in the dream, as in the case of Liu Youqiu seeing his wife in a dream.
  • 愚者少梦，不独至人，问（一云闻）之驺皂，百夕无一梦也 - A statement that foolish people have fewer dreams, not just wise ones, and that stable servants (驺皂) often have no dreams at all.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '鹽，朐腮（一曰肋）縣鹽井有鹽方寸，中央隆起如張傘，名曰傘子鹽。' is descriptive but could be read as either a single statement or a series of related observations about the salt.
  • The sentence '泉，玉門軍有芦葭泉，周二丈，深一丈，驼马千头饮之不竭。' is elliptical but clearly describes the spring's size and abundance.
  • The sentence '伏苓，沈約謝始安王賜伏苓一枚，重十二斤八兩，有表。' is elliptical but clearly describes the gift and its weight.
  • The sentence '古鍋，虢州陵縣石城岗有古鍋一口，樹生其内，大數圍。' is elliptical but clearly describes the ancient pot and the tree growing inside it.
  • The sentence '君王鹽，白鹽崖有鹽如水精，名為君王鹽。' is elliptical but clearly describes the salt and its name.
  • The sentence '手板，宋山陽王休，屢以言語忤顏。有庾道敏者，善相手板。休以己手板托言他人者，庾曰：“此板乃貴，然使人多忤。”休以褚渊詳密，乃換其手板。別日，褚於帝前稱下官，帝甚不悅。' is complex and involves multiple actions and characters, but it can be read as a sequence of events related to the handboard.
  • The sentence '鼠丸，王肃造逐鼠丸，以铜为之，昼夜自转。' is elliptical but clearly describes the device and its function.
  • The sentence '木囚，《论衡》言，李子长为政，欲知囚情。以梧桐为人，象囚之形，凿地为臼，以芦苇为郭，藉卧木囚于其中。囚当罪，木囚不动。囚或冤，木囚乃奋起。' is elliptical but clearly describes the wooden figure and its use in determining guilt or innocence.

  • 滕景真在广州七层寺，元徽中，罢职归家，婢炊，釜中忽有声如雷，米上隆起 - Describes a sudden and loud noise in the pot during cooking, followed by rice sprouting or rising.
  • 滕就视，声转壮，甑上花生数十，渐长似莲花，色赤，有光似金，俄顷萎灭 - The phenomenon intensifies, with many rice-like flowers growing on the steamer, resembling lotuses, turning red, and emitting a golden light before withering quickly.
  • 官金中蝼顶金最上，六两为一垛，有卧蝼蛄穴及水皋形，当中陷处名曰趾腹 - Describes a type of official gold, specifically the 'lou ding' gold, which is the highest quality. It is shaped like a sleeping cricket burrow or a water bank, with a depression in the center called 'zhi fu'.
  • 铤上凹处有紫色，名紫胆 - The depression on the gold bar has a purple color, known as 'zi dan'.
  • 开元中，有大唐金（一有“印”字），即官金也 - During the Kaiyuan period, there was a type of official gold called 'Da Tang Jin' (which might also be referred to as 'Da Tang Yin').
  • 玄金，唐太宗时，汾州言青龙白虎吐物在空中，有光如火，坠地陷入二尺 - Describes a mysterious object that appears in the sky, resembling a dragon and a tiger, with a fiery glow, and then falls to the ground, sinking two feet into the earth.
  • 掘之，得玄金，广尺余，高七寸 - Upon digging, a piece of 'xuan jin' (mysterious gold) is found, measuring over a foot wide and seven inches high.
  • 芝，天保初，临川人李嘉胤所居柱上生芝草，状如天尊，太守张景佚拔柱献焉 - Describes a miraculous mushroom growing on a pillar in the home of Li Jiayin in Linchuan, which resembles a deity, and is subsequently cut down and presented by the governor Zhang Jingyi.
  • 龟，建中四年，赵州宁晋县沙河北，有大棠梨树。百姓常祈祷，忽有群蛇数十，自东南来，渡北岸，集棠梨树下为二积，留南岸者为一积 - Describes a large pear tree near the Yellow River in Ningjin County, Shaozhou, where villagers pray, and suddenly a group of snakes appear, forming two piles on the north bank and one on the south bank.
  • 俄见三龟径寸，绕行积傍，积蛇尽死。乃各登其积，视蛇腹各有疮，若矢所中 - Three turtles, each about an inch in diameter, appear and walk around the piles, causing all the snakes to die. The turtles then climb onto the piles, revealing that the snakes' bellies have wounds as if shot by arrows.
  • 刺史康日知图甘棠奉三龟来献 - The governor Kang Rizhi draws a picture of the pear tree and the three turtles and presents it to the court.

  • 平地深尺余 - Literal meaning 'the snow was over a foot deep on level ground,' describing a heavy snowfall.
  • 大如指，长寸许 - Literal meaning 'about the size of a finger and about an inch long,' describing the size of wooden objects that rained down.
  • 每木有孔通中，所下其立如植 - Describes the characteristics of the wooden objects: each piece had a hole through the center and stood upright as if planted.
  • 色绀光润，随人罪福影其上 - Describes the appearance of the stone pillar: it has a dark blue color, is shiny, and casts shadows that reflect the good or bad deeds of people.
  • 后寇至，鼓辄自鸣 - Describes the subsequent events where the drum indeed sounded when enemies arrived, fulfilling the prophecy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贞元 - The reign title of Emperor Dezong of Tang, covering the years 785-805 AD.
  • 长安 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty, present-day Xi'an.
  • 陈留 - A city in Henan Province, historically significant during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 梵那衍国 - A region in Central Asia, often referred to as Vajrayana or Tibet in historical texts.
  • 金轮王 - A title given to Buddhist kings, particularly those who rule over a vast territory.
  • 劫化他国 - Another name for Vajrayana, indicating a different name for the same region.
  • 无忧王 - The Chinese name for King Asoka, a famous Indian emperor known for his support of Buddhism.
  • 于阗 - An ancient kingdom in what is now Xinjiang, known for its cultural and religious significance.
  • 罗洪僧 - A monk whose name is used to indicate a religious figure in the story.
  • 旃檀鼓 - A sandalwood drum, often associated with religious or ceremonial purposes in Buddhist contexts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 破之，有禄马迹 - "破之" means 'when broken open,' and '禄马迹' refers to a trace or mark of a horse, possibly a symbol of wealth or status.
  • 舍利 - Refers to relics or remains of the Buddha, often in the form of bone fragments or ashes.
  • 如缀珠幡 - Describes the relics as resembling beads on a banner, likely a metaphor for their appearance or arrangement.
  • 啮石壁如雕镌 - "啮" means 'to gnaw,' and '雕镌' means 'to carve,' suggesting that the object is gradually forming a shape through natural processes.
  • 服一粒，永不患疟 - "服" means 'to take,' and '永不患疟' means 'never suffer from malaria,' suggesting a magical or medicinal property.
  • 唯瓢不漏 - "唯" means 'only,' and '瓢' refers to a gourd or calabash, suggesting that only this material can contain the water.
  • 频笑辄落 - "频笑" means 'frequent smiling,' and '辄落' means 'and fall off,' suggesting that the person-like object disappears or is destroyed when it smiles repeatedly.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俱位國以馬種莳 - Refers to the country of Juiwei cultivating horse breeds, indicating a place known for its horse breeding practices.
  • 大食国马解人语 - Indicates that the horses of the Great Food (Taizhi) country understand human language, suggesting a miraculous or highly intelligent trait.
  • 石人 - Refers to stone statues or figures.
  • 莱子国海上有石人 - Describes a geographical feature of stone people on the sea of Lai Zi country.
  • 长一丈五尺，大十围 - Provides specific measurements for the stone figure: one and a half zhang long, ten chi in circumference.
  • 秦始皇遣此石人，追劳山不得，遂立于此 - Historical allusion to Emperor Qin Shi Huang sending stone people to chase Mount Laoshan but failing, so they were placed there.
  • 铜马 - Refers to a bronze statue of a horse.
  • 俱德建国鸟浒河中滩派中有火祆祠 - Describes a specific location and religious site: in the middle of the riverbank of the Bird Hu River in the country of Juede, there is a fire temple.
  • 相传祆神本自波斯国乘神通来此 - Explains the origin of the fire god: it is said that the fire god originally came from the country of Persia with supernatural powers.
  • 常见灵异，因立祆祠 - Indicates the reason for the construction of the temple: frequently seen miraculous phenomena.
  • 内无象，于大屋下置大小炉，舍檐向西，人向东礼 - Describes the layout and rituals of the temple: inside there is no image, but large and small furnaces are placed under the big house, with the eaves facing west and people worshipping facing east.
  • 有一铜马，大如次马，国人言自天下，屈前脚在空中而对神立，后脚入土 - Describes a miraculous bronze horse: as large as a regular horse, said by the locals to have come from heaven, with its front legs bent in the air and standing facing the god, and its hind legs buried in the ground.
  • 西域以五月为岁，每岁日，鸟浒河中有马出，其色如金，与此铜马嘶相应，俄复入水 - Describes a yearly phenomenon: in the Western Regions, May is considered the beginning of the year, and every year on this day, a golden-colored horse appears in the Bird Hu River, echoing the sound of the bronze horse, and then disappears back into the water.
  • 近有大食王不信，入祆祠，将坏之，忽有火烧其兵，遂不敢毁 - Describes a recent event: recently, a king of the Great Food country did not believe it and entered the fire temple to destroy it, but suddenly a fire burned his soldiers, so he dared not destroy it.
  • 蛇碛 - Refers to a sandy area infested with snakes.
  • 苏都瑟匿国西北有蛇碛 - Describes a geographical feature: in the northwest of the country of Sudusen, there is a snake desert.
  • 南北蛇原五百余里，中间遍蛇，毒气如烟 - Describes the dangerous environment: the snake plains stretch for more than five hundred li north and south, with snakes everywhere in the middle, and poisonous gas like smoke.
  • 飞鸟坠地，蛇因吞食 - Describes the natural behavior of snakes: flying birds fall to the ground, and snakes eat them.
  • 或大小相噬，及食生草 - Describes the feeding habits of snakes: sometimes they eat each other, and sometimes they eat fresh grass.
  • 石鼍 - Refers to a stone statue of a crocodile.
  • 私诃条国金辽山寺中有石鼍 - Describes a specific location and object: in the Jin Liao Mountain temple in the country of Siketiao, there is a stone crocodile.
  • 众僧饮食将尽，向石鼍作礼，于是饮食悉具 - Describes a miraculous event: when the monks' food was about to run out, they bowed to the stone crocodile, and then the food was replenished.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俱振提國尚鬼神 - A phrase describing a country that venerates ghosts and spirits, indicating a strong belief in the supernatural.
  • 春秋祠之 - Refers to the practice of offering sacrifices to the deity during the spring and autumn seasons.
  • 自然而出 - Means 'emerges naturally,' suggesting a spontaneous and miraculous occurrence.
  • 祠畢亦滅 - Means 'disappears after the sacrifice is completed,' indicating a temporary manifestation.
  • 毒槊 - Refers to a weapon that is poisonous, with no blade and resembling rusted iron.
  • 中人无血而死 - Means 'kills a person without drawing blood,' highlighting the deadly nature of the weapon.
  • 天后使验之，不妄 - Means 'Empress Wu sent someone to verify it, and it was not false,' indicating the authenticity of the phenomenon.
  • 甲 - Refers to a type of armor, specifically a lock armor.
  • 高丽言前燕时白天而落 - Means 'Goguryeo (Korea) says it fell from the sky during the time of the Former Yan,' suggesting a celestial origin.
  • 土槟榔 - Refers to a type of substance found in holes or crevices, resembling betel nuts.
  • 相传蟾蜍矢也 - Means 'it is said to be the droppings of toads,' indicating a folk belief.
  • 不常有之 - Means 'it does not appear often,' suggesting rarity.
  • 主治恶疮 - Means 'used to treat malignant sores,' indicating its medicinal use.
  • 鬼矢 - Refers to a substance that appears in damp, dark places, with a pale yellowish-white color.
  • 主疮 - Means 'used to treat sores,' indicating its medicinal use.
  • 石栏干 - Refers to a type of stone found at the bottom of the sea, with a height of about one foot.
  • 有根，茎上有孔如物点 - Means 'it has roots, and the stem has holes like marks made by objects,' describing its physical characteristics.
  • 渔人纲育取之 - Means 'fishermen catch it using nets,' indicating how it is harvested.
  • 初出水正红色，见风渐渐青色 - Means 'when first taken out of the water, it is a bright red color, but gradually turns blue when exposed to the wind,' describing its color change.
  • 主石淋 - Means 'used to treat kidney stones,' indicating its medicinal use.
  • 壁影 - Refers to a shadow that appears on the wall of a temple.
  • 每日晚，人马车舆影悉透壁上 - Means 'every evening, the shadows of people, horses, carriages, and carts pass through the wall,' describing the phenomenon.
  • 衣红紫者，影中卤莽可辨 - Means 'those wearing red or purple can be vaguely distinguished in the shadow,' indicating the visibility of certain details.
  • 壁厚数尺，难以理究 - Means 'the wall is several feet thick, making it difficult to explain the phenomenon,' suggesting the mystery of the occurrence.
  • 辰午之时则无 - Means 'it does not appear during the hours of辰 (Chen) and午 (Wu),' indicating specific times when the phenomenon does not occur.
  • 相传如此二十余年矣，或一年半年不见 - Means 'it has been like this for more than twenty years, sometimes not appearing for a year or half a year,' indicating the intermittent nature of the phenomenon.
  • 成式太和初扬州见寄客及僧说 - Means 'Cheng Shi heard about it from guests and monks in Yangzhou during the early Tahe period,' providing the source of the information.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大虫皮 - Literal phrase meaning 'tiger skin,' but likely refers to a rare or mythical creature's skin.
  • 旧有大虫皮，大如一掌，须尾斑点如犬者 - Describes an ancient tiger skin that is large like a hand, with whiskers and tail spots like a dog. This is an unusual description, as tiger skins typically have distinct patterns.
  • 人腊 - Could refer to a human mummy or a wax figure of a person.
  • 长三寸余，头项<骨中>肋成就，云是憔侥国人 - Describes a human wax figure that is three inches long, with a head and ribcage that are well-formed, and is said to be from the Qiao Jiao (焦侥) people, a mythical race.
  • 牛黄 - Refers to the gallstones found in cows, often used in traditional medicine.
  • 牛有黄者，或吐弄之 - Describes a cow that has yellow gallstones, which it may expel or play with.
  • 集贤校书张希复言 - Refers to Zhang Xifu, a scholar and proofreader at the Imperial Library, who is recounting a story.
  • 尝有人得其所吐黄，剖之，中有物如蝶飞去 - Describes a person who obtained the expelled yellow gallstone, cut it open, and found a butterfly-like creature inside that flew away.
  • 上清珠 - Refers to a precious pearl or gemstone.
  • 永宁王盐铁 - Refers to the salt and iron official of the Yongning Prince.
  • 旧有大虫皮 - Repeats the description of the ancient tiger skin.
  • 上清玉珠 - Refers to a jade pearl, likely a precious stone.
  • 以绛纱裹之，系于颈 - Describes the pearl being wrapped in a red silk cloth and tied around the neck.
  • 开元中宾国所贡 - Refers to the pearl being a tribute from the country of Tabgach (大食) during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 光明洁白，可照一室 - Describes the pearl as bright and white, capable of illuminating a room.
  • 视之，则仙人玉女、云鹤降节之形摇动于其中 - Describes the appearance of the pearl, which shows images of immortals, jade maidens, clouds, and cranes moving inside.
  • 及即位，宝库中往往有神光 - Describes the appearance of divine light in the treasure vault after the emperor ascended the throne.
  • 异日掌库者具以事告 - Refers to the keeper of the treasure vault reporting the incident.
  • 岂非上清珠耶 - A rhetorical question asking if it is not the Shangqing Pearl.
  • 遂令出之，绛纱犹在 - Describes the emperor ordering the pearl to be brought out, and the red silk cloth is still intact.
  • 因流泣遍示近臣曰 - Describes the emperor shedding tears and showing the pearl to his close ministers.
  • 此我为儿时，明皇所赐也 - The emperor says that the pearl was a gift from Emperor Xuanzong when he was a child.
  • 遂令贮之以翠玉函，置之于卧内 - Describes the emperor ordering the pearl to be stored in a jade box and placed in his bedroom.
  • 四方忽有水旱兵革之灾 - Refers to natural disasters or military conflicts occurring in various regions.
  • 则虔恳祝之，无不应验也 - Describes the emperor praying sincerely to the pearl, and it always responds to his prayers.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大虫 - A term for a large, fearsome beast, often used to refer to tigers or other mythical creatures.
  • 人腊 - Could refer to a human mummy or a wax figure of a person.
  • 憔侥国 - A mythical race mentioned in ancient Chinese texts, known for their small stature.
  • 牛黄 - A traditional Chinese medicine ingredient derived from the gallstones of cows.
  • 上清珠 - A precious pearl or gemstone, often associated with spiritual or magical properties.
  • 永宁王 - A prince, likely a member of the imperial family.
  • 盐铁 - A high-ranking official responsible for salt and iron, important resources in ancient China.
  • 开元 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for its prosperity and cultural achievements.
  • 宾国 - A reference to the country of Tabgach (大食), a powerful state in Central Asia during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 明皇 - A respectful term for Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for his patronage of the arts and his relationship with Yang Guifei.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 大虫皮，永宁王盐铁，旧有大虫皮，大如一掌，须尾斑点如犬者 - This sentence is elliptical and could be read as: 'The salt and iron official of the Yongning Prince had an ancient tiger skin, large like a hand, with whiskers and tail spots like a dog.'
  • 人腊，李章武有人腊，长三寸余，头项<骨中>肋成就，云是憔侥国人 - This sentence could be read as: 'There was a human wax figure in Li Zhangwu, three inches long, with a well-formed head and ribcage, said to be from the Qiao Jiao people.'
  • 牛黄，牛黄在胆中，牛有黄者，或吐弄之 - This sentence could be read as: 'The yellow gallstone is found in the cow's gallbladder, and a cow with yellow gallstones may expel or play with it.'
  • 上清珠，肃宗为儿时，常为玄宗所器。每坐于前，熟视其貌，谓武惠妃曰：'此儿甚有异相，他日亦吾家一有福天子。'因命取上清玉珠，以绛纱裹之，系于颈 - This sentence could be read as: 'The Shangqing Pearl. When Emperor Suzong was a child, he was often favored by Emperor Xuanzong. Whenever he sat in front of him, Emperor Xuanzong would look at his face and say to Empress Wu Huifei, 'This child has an extraordinary appearance; one day he will also be a fortunate emperor of our family.' Therefore, he ordered the Shangqing Jade Pearl to be taken, wrapped in red silk, and tied around his neck.'
  • 及即位，宝库中往往有神光 - This sentence could be read as: 'After he ascended the throne, divine light often appeared in the treasure vault.'

  • 大虫 - A term for a large, fearsome beast.
  • 永宁王 - A prince, likely a member of the imperial family.
  • 盐铁 - A high-ranking official responsible for salt and iron.
  • 李章武 - A place or person, likely a reference to a specific location or individual.
  • 憔侥国 - A mythical race.
  • 牛黄 - A traditional Chinese medicine ingredient.
  • 上清珠 - A precious pearl or gemstone.
  • 肃宗 - Emperor Suzong of Tang.
  • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
  • 武惠妃 - Empress Wu Huifei, a concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 开元 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 宾国 - A reference to the country of Tabgach (大食).
  • 明皇 - A respectful term for Emperor Xuanzong.
